perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the disclosure of Confidential Information by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party solely for the Purpose, the Receiving Party undertakes whether by itself, its successors and heirs, not to disclose Confidential Information to any third party, unless in accordance with Clause 4.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the disclosure of Confidential Information by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party solely for the Purpose, the Receiving Party will endeavor, whether by itself, its successors and heirs, not to disclose Confidential Information to any third party, unless in accordance with Clause 4.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'undertakes' to 'will endeavor' weakens the obligation, making it a goal rather than a strict requirement. This is an ambiguous obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 72 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 72 of the Indian Contract Act implies that when a party has received an advantage or benefit under a contract, they are legally bound to restore it or compensate for it if the contract becomes void or is discovered to be voidable. By changing the wording to 'will endeavor', the Receiving party can claim that they tried to protect the information, but due to circumstances they were not able to. This creates legal ambiguity and is not a clear, legally binding obligation to protect the confidential information, which is what would be considered as an advantage or benefit under Section 72. It creates loopholes that could be abused.

perturbation.justification: The change from "undertakes" to "will endeavor" significantly weakens the obligation on the Receiving Party to protect Confidential Information. The original text creates a clear and legally binding obligation, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity, allowing the Receiving Party to argue they made an effort but were unable to fulfill the obligation. The Law Explanation provided suggests this shift could allow the Receiving party to abuse loopholes and avoid responsibility, undermining the purpose of protecting the confidential information, even if the Snippets provided were not helpful.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that such Affiliates or employees are subject to confidentiality obligations no less restrictive than those of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will encourage its Affiliates or employees to respect confidentiality obligations similar to those of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees that such Affiliates or employees are subject to confidentiality obligations no less restrictive' to 'will encourage its Affiliates or employees to respect confidentiality obligations similar to those' significantly weakens the obligation. 'Encourage' and 'respect' are not legally binding terms, and 'similar to' introduces ambiguity about the level of confidentiality required. The Receiving party only needs to ask the employee/affiliate to sign something, which is not the same as being subjected to confidentiality obligations no less restrictive than those of this agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 182 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 182 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, defines an 'agent' as a person employed to do any act for another or to represent another in dealings with third persons. By weakening the obligation to 'encourage its Affiliates or employees to respect confidentiality obligations similar to those', the Receiving Party does not ensure that the affiliates and employees are bounded by the same contract as the Recipient party, causing ambiguity and violating the legal obligation. This can create a loophole if the affiliates/employees are acting as agents for the receiving party but not bound by the original contract, thus violating the legal obligations for the Recipient party.
perturbation.location: IV. Permitted Disclosure of Confidential Information: 1

All states Alabama Alaska Arizona Arkansas California Colorado Connecticut Delaware District of Columbia Florida Georgia Hawaii Idaho Illinois Indiana Iowa Kansas Kentucky Louisiana Maine Maryland Massachusetts Michigan Minnesota Mississippi Missouri Montana Nebraska Nevada New Hampshire New Jersey New Mexico New York North Carolina North Dakota Ohio Oklahoma Oregon Pennsylvania Rhode Island South Carolina South Dakota Tennessee Texas Utah Vermont Virginia Washington West Virginia Wisconsin Wyoming Select your State Alabama Alaska Arizona Arkansas California Colorado Connecticut Delaware District of Columbia Florida Georgia Hawaii Idaho Illinois Indiana Iowa Kansas Kentucky Louisiana Maine Maryland Massachusetts Michigan Minnesota Mississippi Missouri Montana Nebraska Nevada New Hampshire New Jersey New Mexico New York North Carolina North Dakota Ohio Oklahoma Oregon Pennsylvania Rhode Island South Carolina South Dakota Tennessee Texas Utah Vermont Virginia Washington West Virginia Wisconsin Wyoming Search Popular forms Show Last Will and Testament Power of Attorney Promissory Note LLC Operating Agreement Living Will Rental Lease Agreement Non-Disclosure Agreement Conspiracy Law and Legal Definition Conspiracy is a separate offense, by which someone conspires or agrees with someone else to do something which, if actually carried out, would amount to another federal crime or offense. It is an agreement or a kind of partnership for criminal purposes in which each member becomes the agent or partner of every other member. It is not necessary to prove that the criminal plan actually was accomplished or that the conspirator was involved in all stages of the planning or knew all of the details involved. The main elements that need to be proven are a voluntary agreement to participate and some overt act by one of the conspirators in furtherance of the criminal plan. If a person has an understanding of the unlawful nature of a plan and knowingly and willfully joins in that plan on one occasion, that is sufficient to convict him for conspiracy even though he had not participated before and even though he played only a minor part. A conspiracy may exist when the parties use legal means to accomplish an illegal result, or to use illegal means to achieve something that in itself is lawful. Wheel and chain conspiracies are two types of conspiracies described in prosecuting offenders. A chain conspiracy involves parties linked together in a linear fashion. Typical drug or firearm smuggling organizations are chain conspiracies. It may consist of a series of drug deals, from manufacturer to the street dealer. In a wheel conspiracy, the ringleader is the "hub" and subsidiary parties are the "spokes". It is generally easier for prosecution to prove that a "chain" constituted a single conspiracy than it is to prove that a "wheel" was a single organization Legal Definition list Conspicuous Term Conspicuous Consortship Consortium/Third party administrator [Shipping] Consortium Vitae Conspiracy Conspiracy to Induce Breach of Contract Conspirator Constable Constituent Corporation Constitution Related Legal Terms Bathtub Conspiracy Chain Conspiracy CIA Conspiracy Theory Civil Conspiracy Civil Rights Conspiracy Conspiracy to Induce Breach of Contract Criminal Conspiracy Defense, Withdrawal from Conspiracy Dragnet of Conspiracy Federal Reserve Conspiracy
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures affiliates and employees are 'subject to confidentiality obligations no less restrictive' than the agreement, establishing a clear legal obligation. The changed text weakens this by stating the Receiving Party 'will encourage its Affiliates or employees to respect confidentiality obligations similar to those,' which is not legally binding. This creates ambiguity and potential non-compliance, as 'encourage' and 'respect' are not enforceable terms. The LLM explanation points to how this weakening can create a loophole with agents. The second law snippet discusses conspiracy, where agreements to commit unlawful acts are considered offenses, underscoring the importance of enforceable agreements, which the changed text fails to provide. This introduces a clear ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of confidentiality, creating a legal contradiction as the affiliates/employees are no longer necessarily bound by the original contract's confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall limit the dissemination of Confidential Information of its Affiliates and employees having a need to receive such information to carry out the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will try to limit the dissemination of Confidential Information of its Affiliates and employees deemed important to receive such information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall limit the dissemination' with 'will try to limit the dissemination' makes the obligation less strict. Replacing 'having a need to receive such information to carry out the Purpose' with 'deemed important to receive such information' introduces subjective judgment and makes the criteria for dissemination ambiguous. 'Try' and 'deemed important' weaken the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 43A of the Information Technology Act, 2000, pertains to compensation for failure to protect data. The original text ensures that the dissemination of sensitive information is limited to only personnel that require it. The modified text gives ambiguous authority to whom the information is disseminated to and fails to be compliant with the requirements under Section 43A and could result in a lawsuit. This weakened obligation does not ensure reasonable security practices, potentially leading to a failure to protect sensitive data and triggering liabilities under Section 43A.
perturbation.location: IV. Permitted Disclosure of Confidential Information: 2

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: actus reus | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you actus reus Actus reus refers to the act or omission that comprise the physical elements of a crime as required by statute . Actus reus includes only a voluntary affirmative act , or an omission (failure to act), causing a criminally proscribed result. For example, if a thief shoves a gun into the side of a victim and says: “Your money or your life” - the shoving of the gun is the actus reus. An involuntary act does not satisfy the actus reus requirement. Act The actus reus includes only voluntary bodily movements, particularly one which society has an interest in preventing. This was confirmed by the Supreme Court in Powell v. Texas , 392 U.S. 514 (1968) . Thus, if a defendant acted on reflex, then the defendant's conduct does not satisfy the actus reus requirement. Contrast this with mens rea , which refers to the criminal intent element of a crime. Omission Alternately, the actus reus requirement can also be satisfied by an omission. This is true only when the individual had a duty to act, and failed to act. Generally, for the purposes of criminal liability , an individual may be under a duty to act if: A statute requires a person to act in a certain way. A contract requires a person to act in a certain way. Some special status relationship exists that creates a duty to act in a certain way (i.e. parental responsibilities). A voluntary assumption of care creates a duty to act in a certain way. The individual created the risk. [Last reviewed in June of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords criminal law mens rea mental state criminal act Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS trial process/advocacy THE LEGAL PROCESS criminal law criminal procedure evidence courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the intention of Section 43A of the Information Technology Act, 2000, regarding data protection. The original text mandates limiting dissemination to those with a 'need' related to the Purpose. The change to 'will try' weakens the obligation. The change to 'deemed important' introduces subjective judgment, potentially broadening access beyond those with a legitimate need and creating ambiguity. While scraped snippet 1 is a dead link, scraped snippet 2 defines 'actus reus' and highlights the importance of 'voluntary affirmative act' and 'duty to act', which in the context of data security, relates to the company's active and responsible handling of sensitive information. The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the 'duty to act' responsibly, potentially leading to failure to protect sensitive data. This creates a significant contradiction, as it makes compliance with stringent data protection standards less certain.
